per_question.question: What is the time complexity of a single for loop that runs from 1 to n?
per_question.ground_truth: A single for loop running from 1 to n has a time complexity of O(n), because the loop body executes exactly n times proportional to the input size.

per_question.question: What is the time complexity of two nested for loops each running from 1 to n?
per_question.ground_truth: Two nested for loops each running from 1 to n have a time complexity of O(n squared), because the inner loop executes n times for each of the n iterations of the outer loop, giving n times n total operations.

per_question.question: What happens to the time complexity when a loop variable is doubled each iteration instead of incremented?
per_question.ground_truth: When a loop variable is doubled each iteration, the loop runs approximately log base 2 of n times, giving a time complexity of O(log n), because doubling reduces the number of iterations logarithmically.

per_question.question: What is the time complexity of a loop where the outer loop runs n times and the inner loop runs log n times?
per_question.ground_truth: When the outer loop runs n times and the inner loop runs log n times, the combined time complexity is O(n log n), which is the product of the two independent loop counts.

per_question.question: How do you determine the time complexity of a loop where the iteration count depends on dividing by a constant factor each time?
per_question.ground_truth: When a loop divides its counter by a constant factor each iteration, the number of iterations is logarithmic. If the factor is 2, it runs log base 2 of n times, giving O(log n) complexity.

per_question.question: What is the time complexity when three nested loops each iterate n times?
per_question.ground_truth: Three nested loops each iterating n times produce a time complexity of O(n cubed), because each nesting level multiplies the iteration count by n.

per_question.question: What is the difference between O(n) and O(n squared) in terms of algorithm performance?
per_question.ground_truth: O(n) grows linearly with input size while O(n squared) grows quadratically. For large inputs, an O(n squared) algorithm becomes significantly slower, for example at n equals 1000, O(n) performs 1000 operations while O(n squared) performs 1 million operations.

per_question.question: How is the complexity of consecutive independent loops calculated?
per_question.ground_truth: The complexity of consecutive independent loops is calculated by taking the maximum of their individual complexities. For example, a loop with O(n) followed by a loop with O(n squared) gives an overall complexity of O(n squared), not their sum.

per_question.question: What does it mean for an algorithm to have O(1) constant time complexity?
per_question.ground_truth: O(1) constant time complexity means the algorithm takes the same amount of time regardless of input size. The number of operations does not grow with n.

per_question.question: How is the complexity analysed when a loop runs from i equals 1 to n and an inner statement runs i times?
per_question.ground_truth: When the outer loop runs from 1 to n and an inner statement runs i times, the total operations are the sum from 1 to n which equals n times n plus 1 divided by 2, giving a time complexity of O(n squared).

per_question.question: What is the time complexity of binary search?
per_question.ground_truth: Binary search has a time complexity of O(log n) because each step halves the search space, resulting in at most log base 2 of n comparisons to find the target element.

per_question.question: What is the time complexity of bubble sort in the worst case?
per_question.ground_truth: Bubble sort has a worst case time complexity of O(n squared) because it uses two nested loops each running up to n times to compare and swap adjacent elements.
